sources_audited.note: The Census page defines 541618 as other management consulting, lists the four excluded consulting categories, and expressly includes telecommunications and utilities management consulting. It supports the industry lens, while the transferability value remains openly classified as an estimate.

The May 2023 OEWS page reports the cited broader-541600 shares: management occupations 16.21%, management analysts 11.91%, project management specialists 3.55%, computer occupations 9.56%, and office and administrative support 14.98%. The packet explicitly discloses that this is a broader-industry proxy rather than a six-digit task measure.

BLS reports 9% projected management-analyst employment growth for 2024-2034, describes the cited interview, observation, analysis, recommendation, presentation, and manager-conference duties, and says consulting demand should grow as organizations pursue efficiency and cost control. The occupation-to-industry and employment-to-output transformations are disclosed as proxy limitations.

The Organization Science article reports a randomized experiment involving 758 BCG consultants, 12.2% more completed inside-frontier tasks, 25.1% faster completion, and a 19% lower probability of a correct solution on the outside-frontier task. The packet appropriately limits this to a proxy for smaller consulting firms and broader workflows.

McKinsey reports 88% regular AI use in at least one function and defines AI high performers, about 6% of respondents, by significant value and at least 5% EBIT impact; it also reports that most organizations remain in pilot or experimentation stages. The packet discloses the survey's mismatch to LMM consulting implementation and uses it only as a proxy.

The cited PDF supports the stated pricing mix (37% project, 26% value-based, 21% hourly, 13% retainer, 3% performance-based) and the statement that over 80% lowered fees to win a client. It does not contain the separate claim used in the e rationale that high-performing firms earn 70% of revenue from existing clients. That statistic appears on a different Consulting Success webpage, not the cited PDF.

Gallup reports a 1,264-owner-worker survey and, for employer owners' five-year plans, 13% planning to sell, 11% to transfer, and a 22% sell/transfer/public summary; it also reports Census data that 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people 55 or older. The packet openly discounts plans, gifts, and the broad population when estimating completed qualifying transfers.

FOCUS reports 466 announced US professional-services transactions in the first nine months of 2025 and that consulting plus accounting represented more than 50% of sector deal volume. The packet correctly describes these as announced, broad-sector transactions and not completed qualifying 541618 transfers.

caveats: S6 does not support the e rationale's claim that high-performing firms earn 70% of revenue from existing clients; the cited PDF is a pricing study, while that statistic appears on a different Consulting Success webpage. Removing the statement would weaken the narrative basis for e but would not plausibly push e below the roughly 0.31 level needed to move F under 4, and e is already an ESTIMATE, so neither the PRIORITY base tier nor VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action changes.

The evidence for a is a disclosed combination of an occupation mix for broad NAICS 541600 and selected BCG tasks rather than a wage-weighted task inventory for NAICS 541618. The 0.34-0.61 range is judgmental, but the high frozen l makes H remain at or above the PRIORITY threshold throughout the registered range.

The 57% rho base extrapolates from enterprise-wide AI adoption and a top-tier consultancy experiment to five-year avoided-labor implementation in smaller firms. The bridge states the relevant confidentiality, workflow, scaling, and firm-size error directions, but no source measures the target construct directly.

The s5 evidence combines broad employer-owner intentions with announced professional-services deal volume, neither of which measures completed control transfers in the stated NAICS and EBITDA band. The record clearly discounts and labels that transformation as a proxy, and F's registered range remains above 4.

The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio of 1.3991 is economically anomalous and caps H at 10 in the base case; the packet and memo disclose the vintage mismatch and harmonization, and methodology holds this dataset anchor fixed outside the research-input envelope.
summary: Publishable with caveats. Mechanics pass, all eight score-driving sources are reachable, evidence states and proxy bridges are generally candid, the default lens is coherent, and the conclusions follow from the registered evidence. S6 is only partial support because its PDF does not contain the 70% existing-client statistic used in the e rationale, but correcting that citation does not pass the methodology's materiality test. The record remains useful as a model-conditioned screen with substantial disclosed uncertainty in the labor anchor, LMM implementation, transfer frequency, and six-digit construct fit.
